title: Variables
description: Give a value a name so you can use it again.

concept.title: Variables help JavaScript remember
concept.body: A variable is like a labeled box. JavaScript gives us three ways to create variables: let, const, and var.
concept.simple: Use let when values can change. Use const when values should stay the same. You may still see var in older JavaScript code.
concept.tip: Use const by default. Use let when the value needs to change. You'll mostly see var in older code.

mistake.title: JavaScript cares about capital letters
mistake.why: score and Score look similar, but JavaScript sees two different names.

challenge.hint: Type the number 5. Numbers do not need quotes.

quiz.question: Which keyword is best for a value that should never change?

title: Primitive DataTypes
description: Tell words, numbers, and true-or-false values apart and more.

concept.title: Values come in different types
concept.body: Primitive types are the basic building blocks of JavaScript. The most common ones are String, Number, and Boolean.
concept.simple: Strings store text. Numbers store numbers. Booleans store true or false.
concept.tip: Strings wear quotes. Numbers and booleans do not.

mistake.title: Words need quotation marks
mistake.why: Without quotes, JavaScript thinks pizza is a variable name.

challenge.hint: Put hello between double quotation marks.

title: More Primitive Types
description: Meet undefined, null, BigInt and Symbol.

concept.title: Not every value is text or a number
concept.body: JavaScript also has undefined, null, BigInt and Symbol.
concept.simple: Some values represent emptiness, huge numbers, or unique identifiers.
concept.tip: Don't worry about memorizing Symbol yet. Just recognize it exists.

mistake.title: Undefined and null are different
mistake.why: undefined means no value has been assigned yet. null means intentionally empty.

quiz.question: Which primitive type stores very large numbers?

concept.title: Some values can hold many things
concept.body: Arrays, objects and functions help us organize more complex information.
concept.simple: Arrays hold lists. Objects hold information. Functions hold actions.
concept.tip: Most real-world JavaScript programs use arrays and objects every day.

challenge.prompt: How many items are inside this array?

quiz.question: Which datatype stores a list of values?

title: Operators
description: Use symbols to calculate, compare and make decisions.

concept.title: Operators help JavaScript do work
concept.body: Operators are special symbols that perform actions. Some do math, some compare values, some combine conditions and some make decisions.
concept.simple: Arithmetic operators do math. Comparison operators ask questions. Logical operators combine conditions. Ternary operators make quick decisions.
concept.tip: Use === instead of == whenever possible because it checks both value and datatype.

mistake.title: == and === are different
mistake.why: == converts datatypes automatically. === checks both value and datatype.

challenge.prompt: Choose the operator that adds 4 and 2.

quiz.question: Which operator checks both value and datatype?

title: Conditions
description: Let your code choose what to do next.

concept.title: Conditions help code decide
concept.body: An if statement asks a true-or-false question. When the answer is true, its code runs.
concept.simple: It is like asking: Is it raining? If yes, take an umbrella.
concept.tip: The code inside the curly braces runs only when the question is true.

mistake.title: Use the exact same capital letters
mistake.why: Ready is not the same name as ready.

challenge.prompt: Make the condition check if lives is above 0.

quiz.question: When does code inside an if statement run?

title: Loops
description: Repeat an action without writing it again and again.

concept.title: Loops repeat code for you
concept.body: A loop runs the same code more than once. This loop counts from 1 to 3.
concept.simple: A loop is like saying, Clap three times, instead of writing clap three separate times.
concept.tip: count++ adds one after each turn.

mistake.title: A missing semicolon can be confusing
mistake.why: JavaScript often guesses correctly, but semicolons clearly show where instructions end.

challenge.hint: Type the final number you want to print.

title: Functions
description: Save a set of instructions and use it anytime.

concept.title: Functions are mini recipes
concept.body: A function groups instructions under one name. Use that name with () to run the instructions.
concept.simple: A function is a button you build. Give it a name, then press it by writing its name with ().
concept.tip: First make the function. Then call it.

mistake.title: Do not forget the parentheses
mistake.why: Writing cheer names the function. Adding () runs it.

challenge.hint: Type the function name before the parentheses.
